segments.s135-g41: سخنهای این داستان = 'the words/verses of this tale' (سخنهای is plural 'words/verses', modified by این داستان). شد is a past intransitive 'became/came' used here to mean 'came to' or 'reached'; به + بن is a prepositional phrase 'to the root/foundation' (بن = core, essence). Together شد به بن is a somewhat idiomatic poetic way to say the narrative has reached its essential point or culmination.
segments.s135-g42: ز introduces the topic/source ('about' or 'concerning' in poetic register). سهراب و رستم is the coordinated noun phrase 'Sohrab and Rostam'. سرایم is 1st person singular present-subjunctive form of سرودن/سراییدن used here with a future/intentional force ('I will compose/sing'). سخن is the object 'poem/words' and appears after the verb by poetic inversion: 'ز سهراب و رستم سرایم سخن' = 'I will compose a poem about Sohrab and Rostam.'
segments.s135-g17: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. لشکر is the noun 'host/army'. هرانکس که introduces a free relative clause 'whoever/whosoever'. بد modifies جنگ‌ساز and here functions as an adjective/adverb meaning 'bad/poorly (suited)', so بد جنگ‌ساز = 'a poor/ill-made war‑maker' (i.e. a bad fighter or warmonger). The whole segment sets up the semantic subject of the next clause.
segments.s135-g18: دو بهره is the object 'two shares' or 'a twofold share'. نیامد is past tense negative 'did not come' (intransitive here: 'did not arrive/was not given'). به marks direction/beneficiary 'to/for', خرگاه is the place 'stable/lowly lodging', and باز after a place can mean 'back/again' — together 'did not receive the twofold share back to the stable' (i.e. the two shares did not come back with him). Word order is somewhat poetic: the logical subject (whoever...) was expressed previously, while this segment gives what did not come/occur.
segments.s135-g19: اگر ... اگر presents two alternative conditions ('if ... or if ...'). کشته بودند is the compound past passive/participial + auxiliary 'were killed' (کشته = 'killed', بودند = past plural 'were'). The second اگر introduces the alternative 'if (they were) weary-bodied' where خسته تن = 'weary body' (an idiom for an exhausted person). The repetition of اگر signals alternatives rather than emphasis.
segments.s135-g20: گرفتار here is adjective/participle 'caught/captured'. در دست means 'in the hand(s) of' where دست carries the idiomatic sense 'power/control/possession' and آن انجمن is 'that assembly/band (of people)'. Together: 'captured in the hands of that band.' Word order places the predicate (گرفتار) before the agent phrase, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s135-g21: ز marks 'from' as part of a list of spoils. پرمایه is an adjective meaning 'well‑provided' or 'valuable'; here it qualifies اسپان (poetic plural of 'horse'), so پرمایه اسپان = 'well‑bred/valuable horses'. زرین precedes ستام (a poetic/traditional term here for trappings or harness) to mean 'golden trappings' — the exact nuance of ستام is archaic/poetic, but syntactically it is a noun modified by زرین.
segments.s135-g22: Another pair introduced by ز ... و ز = 'from ... and from ...'. ترگ is a small item (here 'rag' or cloth scrap) and شمشیر زرین = 'the golden sword' where زرین modifies شمشیر. نیام in this line is a noun meaning 'sheath/scabbard' (not the verb 'did not come') and attaches to شمشیر زرین to yield 'the sheath of the golden sword'. The segment lists further items taken (or not returned) using repeated 'ز' prepositions.
segments.s135-g23: جزین 'except these' sets up an exclusion. هرچه ... بود is a free relative clause 'whatever/that which was', and پرمایه‌تر is the comparative 'more valuable/more richly provided'. بود is the past copula 'was'. نیز here means 'also' or 'too' and links this clause to the following result clause.
segments.s135-g24: به ایرانیان is a beneficiary/dative 'to the Iranians'. ماند is the past 'remained/was left' (subject-predicate inversion is poetic: the thing that remained is stated after the verb), and بسیار چیز is the quantifier phrase 'many things' (بسیار as 'many' modifying چیز). Together: 'many things remained to/with the Iranians.'
segments.s135-g1: Simple past transitive clause: subject تهمتن precedes the past-tense verb برانگیخت (he roused/spurred). رخش is the direct object (the steed) and از شتاب is an adverbial phrase ('into/with haste'); classical Persian often places the object before or after such adverbials. The verb برانگیخت is a simple past (he roused/caused to move).
segments.s135-g2: A short adverbial/locative phrase modifying the previous action: پس 'then' + پشت 'behind/rear of' + the apposition جنگ‌آور افراسیاب ('the warrior Afrasiab'). There is no finite verb here; the clause is elliptical and understood to continue the situation set up by the previous clause (i.e., he came up behind Afrasiab).
segments.s135-g3: چنین گفت is a fixed narrative phrase 'thus he spoke/said'; با رخش marks the addressee ('to/with Rakhsh'). کای is the poetic vocative 'O', followed by the epithet نیک یار ('good companion'). Word order places the vocative after the address marker, which is common in epic speech.
segments.s135-g4: Imperative negative construction: مکن is the classical negative imperative 'do not (do)'. سستی is the noun 'weakness' and اندر گه کارزار is an adverbial phrase 'in the hour/occasion of battle' (اندر = in, گه = time/occasion, کارزار = battle). The whole yields 'do not show weakness in the hour of battle.'
segments.s135-g5: Subordinate/result clause introduced by که ('that/so that'): من is the first-person subject 'I', شاه را is the definite object 'the king' (را marks definiteness/object), and بر تو + بی‌جان کنم is a causative/result phrase 'I will make (him) lifeless upon/by you' — کنم is the present-future-form of 'to make/do' used here for 'I will cause/make'.
segments.s135-g6: Instrumental/result clause: به خون ('with/by blood' — به used instrumentally) introduces the means, سنگ را رنگ مرجان کنم is a causative formula 'I will make the stone the color of coral' (سنگ را: the stone as definite object; رنگ مرجان: 'the color of coral'; کنم repeats the causative 'I will make'). The two clauses are parallel threats (slaying the king; staining the stone with blood).
segments.s135-g7: Resultative descriptive clause: چنان گرم شد is an intransitive past formation 'became so hot/fierce' (چنان = so/thus; گرم شد = became heated/ardent). رخش is the subject; آتش گهر is a poetic apposition ('fire-gem' / 'jewel of flame') that intensifies the metaphor for the horse's ardor.
segments.s135-g8: Result clause introduced by که 'that' giving a vivid comparison: گفتی is a modal/quotative use meaning 'one would say' or 'you might say', برآمد 'rose/issued forth', ز پهلوش 'from his flank' and پر 'a feather'. The construction expresses a simile — so intense that 'one would say a feather sprang from his side.'
segments.s135-g33: This is a locative+subject phrase. 'بران دشت فرخنده' is an adverbial locative ('on/there, the auspicious plain') and 'بر پهلوان' places the hero at that location with the preposition 'بر' ('on/upon'). In classical Persian the scene-setting prepositional phrase can precede the subject.
segments.s135-g34: 'دو هفته' is a duration phrase ('two weeks') that precedes the copula. 'همی' is an archaic continuous/iterative particle (here meaning 'was/kept being'), combined with the past copula 'بود' to indicate a continued state, and 'روشن‌روان' is the predicate (an epithet: 'bright‑souled'). Word order: duration → aspect particle → copula → predicate.
segments.s135-g35: 'سیم را' is a definite direct object marked by the object marker 'را' (so someone brings or escorts Sim). 'به درگاه شاه' is a prepositional phrase 'to the king's court' placed before the verb; 'آمدند' is the plural past verb 'they came' (here meaning 'they brought/arrived with'). The structure is typical SOV with preposed adverbials.
segments.s135-g36: 'به دیدار' = 'to (a) visit / to see'; it introduces the purpose of motion. 'فرخ کلاه' is a compound name/title functioning as the object of that visit, and the verb 'آمدند' (again plural) states 'they came' — the repeated verb links the two coordinated clauses (they came to the court; they came to see Farakh‑Kolah).
segments.s135-g37: 'چنین است' is a stock formula 'thus is / such is'. 'رسم' (subject) 'the custom/tradition' is followed by a genitive 'سرای سپنج' ('the house/establishment of Sepanj' — a proper/place name in context). Here 'سرای سپنج' is a compound nominal phrase; the copula comes early ('چنین است') for rhetorical effect.
segments.s135-g38: 'یکی زو تن آسان' = 'one of them (یکی ز او/زو) is easy‑bodied' where 'ز' is the poetic form of 'از' ('from/of') and 'تن آسان' is a noun + adjective ('easy-bodied' / comfortable). 'و دیگر به رنج' connects with 'and the other (is) in hardship' — 'به رنج' is a prepositional phrase indicating suffering. Word order places the contrasted subjects first with their predicates following.
segments.s135-g39: 'برین و بران' is a paired demonstrative 'this and that' (here 'برین' = 'this', 'بران' = 'that'). 'روز' is the noun 'day/time'. 'هم' adds 'also/even', and 'بگذرد' (present/subjunctive form) is used poetically to mean 'passes/will pass' — a general truth about the passing of days. Word order: demonstratives + noun + adjunct + verb.
segments.s135-g40: 'خردمند مردم' = 'wise people' (adjective + noun). 'چرا' is the interrogative 'why' introducing a rhetorical question, 'غم' is the object 'sorrow', and 'خورد' here means 'befall / experience' (i.e., 'to be afflicted with sorrow'). The clause asks rhetorically why sensible people should suffer grief.
segments.s135-g25: میان (preposition) + بازنگشاد (adjective, poetic/compounded) + کس کشته را forms an object phrase meaning ‘the slain (person) among the unopened/undeclared [places]’. میان governs the location (‘among/in the midst of’). بازنگشاد is an archaic/poetic adjective ‘unreopened, unrevealed’ modifying the locus; کس کُشته (lit. ‘anyone/one slain’) with را marks the definite object.
segments.s135-g26: مردان برگشته را is the topicalized/emphatic subject (را after a subject is a poetic device here). نجستند is a past plural verb 'they searched/ sought' (here rendered negatively by context: they searched but did not find). The verb agrees with مردان (they, plural); the object (the slain) was given in the previous segment.
segments.s135-g27: بدان دشت ('to that plain') uses an archaic/dense prepositional compound (ب- + دان/آن) to indicate direction/place. نخچیر باز آمدند is a verbal clause: نخچیر here carries the sense of the hunt/booty or hunting-ground in context, and باز آمدند is the past plural verb 'they came back/returned' (باز = again/returned + آمدند). Word order places the place phrase first for emphasis.
segments.s135-g28: ز هر نیکویی بی‌نیاز آمدند is a comment on their condition on return: ز here = 'from/with (poetic)', هر نیکویی = 'every goodness/excellence', بی‌نیاز آمدند = 'they came back self-sufficient/without need'. The verb آمدند is past plural and the adjective بی‌نیاز describes their state on returning.
segments.s135-g29: نوشتند is the simple past plural 'they wrote'; نامه is the direct object 'a letter'; به introduces the indirect object/recipient 'to Kāvūs the king' (proper name + title). Word order is SVO with the recipient marked by به.
segments.s135-g30: ز ترکان وز دشت نخچیرگاه is a terse poetic prepositional phrase specifying the letter's subject/place: ز ترکان = 'concerning/from the Turks' (z = 'from/about' in poetry), وز here functions as a compounded conjunctional preposition (classical poetic usage combining و + ز) to link 'and from', and دشت نخچیرگاه = 'the hunting-plain' (nakhchīr-gāh). The sequence lists the topics/locations reported in the letter.
segments.s135-g31: وزان کز دلیران نشد کشته کس is a compact classical clause: وزان = 'and/from that', کز = relative 'that/from which', دلیران = 'the brave (ones)', نشد here functions as 'did not occur/was not the case', and کشته کس = 'a slain person' (with کس used in the sense 'anyone/no one' depending on negation). Together they mean 'from that (time) it was that none of the brave were killed.'
segments.s135-g32: زواره ز اسپ اندر افتاد و بس names the casualty: زواره (a place-name or personal name) + ز اسپ اندر افتاد = 'fell from his horse' (ز = 'from', اسپ = 'steed/horse', اندر افتاد = 'fell into/from' — اندر is an archaic preposition). و بس is a short closing formula 'and that was enough/that was all'.
segments.s135-g9: ز (z) is the poetic preposition 'from' and governs فتراک (the saddle-cloth). بگشاد is a past tense verb (from گشودن, 'to open/loosen'); in classical word order the verb can precede the subject, so بگشاد رستم کمند reads 'Rostam loosened the lasso (from) the saddle-cloth.' کمند is the direct object 'lasso'.
segments.s135-g10: همی is a classical aspect particle giving a continuing or 'about to' sense; خواست is past 'wanted/tried' used as a light auxiliary meaning 'attempted/was going to'. آورد ('bring') is the lexical verb in the periphrasis, and او را is the definite object with the object marker را. ببند is a jussive/infinitival form 'to bind' (here: 'to bind him'), expressing the intended result.
segments.s135-g11: به ترک 'to/against the Turk' marks direction/target (به + noun). اندر افتاد is a compound: اندر (in/into) + افتاد (fell) giving 'fell upon' or 'swooped on'. خم دوال is a postposed manner phrase: خم 'a curve/bend' qualified by دوال 'twofold/double', describing the double-curved sweep of the movement.
segments.s135-g12: سپهدار ترکان is a genitival noun phrase 'the commander of the Turks' (noun + genitive). بدزدید is a past-tense verb meaning 'snatched' or 'stole' (here contextually 'snatched'), and یال is the direct object 'the mane'. Word order is Subject–Verb–Object in this short clause.
segments.s135-g13: و دیگر 'and the other' introduces a new participant. که is the relative particle 'who/which'. زیر اندرش is a compact locative/possessive expression: زیر 'under' + اندر (here 'in/at') + ش (clitic for 'his') together meaning 'under him' or 'beneath his'. بادپای is an adjective/epithet 'swift-footed' (often describing a horse) modifying the understood mount.
segments.s135-g14: به کردار آتش is a simile phrase: به + کردار ('in the manner/way of') + آتش ('fire') => 'in the manner of fire' (i.e. like fire). برآمد is the past 'arose/sprang up', and ز جای is a prepositional phrase 'from the place' (i.e. from his place). Together: 'sprang up from his place like fire.'
segments.s135-g15: بجست is a past verb 'he leapt' or 'he sprang'. از کمند is the prepositional phrase 'out of the noose' (از = from, کمند = lasso/snare). گو پیلتن is a classical locution 'called Piltan' (گو used to mark a name/epithet + پیلتن the place-name), so the whole means 'he leapt out of the noose to the place called Piltan.'
segments.s135-g16: دهن خشک is a noun + adjective 'mouth dry' (i.e. parched mouth). وز رنج uses an archaic preposition/connector وز here meaning roughly 'from' or 'by reason of' (this sense is not listed in the provided options); رنج is 'suffering/hardship'. پر آب تن is a compact phrase: پر 'full (of)' + آب 'water' + تن 'body' — 'a body full of water' is a poetic way to say the body is wet/soaked (here: sweating from hardship).

sentences.s135-s21: The words of this tale have come to their root; I will compose a poem about Sohrab and Rostam.
sentences.s135-s9: From the host, whoever was a poor war-maker did not receive the twofold share back to the stable.
sentences.s135-s10: If they were slain, or if weary bodies were taken captive in the hands of that band,
sentences.s135-s11: From the well‑bred horses (their) golden trappings, from rags and from the golden swords (their) sheaths,
sentences.s135-s12: Except for these, whatever was more valuable also remained with the Iranians — many things remained.
sentences.s135-s1: Tahmatan spurred Rakhsh into haste, coming up behind the warrior Afrasiab.
sentences.s135-s2: Thus he said to Rakhsh, 'O good companion, do not show weakness in the hour of battle.'
sentences.s135-s3: that I will make the king lifeless by your hand; with blood I will make the stone the color of coral.
sentences.s135-s4: Rakhsh grew so fiery, a jewel of flame, that one would have said a feather sprang from his flank.
sentences.s135-s17: On that auspicious plain the hero remained bright‑souled for two weeks.
sentences.s135-s18: They brought Sim to the king's court; they came to visit Farakh‑Kolah.
sentences.s135-s19: Such is the custom of the house of Sepanj: one of them is easy‑bodied and the other is in hardship.
sentences.s135-s20: This and that day will also pass; the wise among the people, why should they grieve?
sentences.s135-s13: The men who had returned searched but could not find the slain among the unopened (places).
sentences.s135-s14: They returned to that hunting-plain; they came back with every excellence, free of want.
sentences.s135-s15: They wrote a letter to King Kavus about the Turks and about the hunting-ground plain.
sentences.s135-s16: From that (time) it happened that none of the brave were slain; Zowāreh fell from his horse — and that was all.
sentences.s135-s5: Rostam loosened the lasso from the saddle-cloth; he was about to pull it up to bind him.
sentences.s135-s6: He swooped on the Turk with a double-curved sweep; the Turks' commander snatched the mane.
sentences.s135-s7: And the other, whose mount was swift of foot, sprang up from his place like fire.
sentences.s135-s8: He leapt out of the noose to the place called Piltan; his mouth was dry and, from hardship, his body full of water (i.e. soaked with sweat).
